We are a leading player in the Food & FMCG, Home and Personal Care, and Edible Oils sectors, offering products that serve premium to mass market segments across urban and rural India. We cater to a diverse customer base, including institutional buyers and the HoReCa sector. Our products are also exported to 37 countries, reinforcing our growing global presence.
To effectively serve this wide customer base, we have rolled out multiple retail formats to address the evolving needs of discerning consumers. We have established a robust distribution network comprising over 8,000 distributors, 98 sales depots, 2 million+ retail touchpoints, complemented by 1,039 Chikitsalays, 387 Mega Stores, 3,420 Aarogya Kendras, and Grameen Aarogya Kendras, ensuring convenient availability of our products. Our portfolio is accessible through e-commerce platforms and organized retail channels, enhancing market reach. Our “Order Me” app enhances online customer engagement.

In November 2024, Patanjali Foods Ltd. acquired the Home & Personal Care (HPC) business
through a slump sale, expanding our portfolio and reinforcing our commitment to embed
sustainability at the core of our strategy. We set short-, medium-, and long-term internal targets,
reviewing progress periodically to drive continuous improvement

Key Sustainability Highlights (FY 2024-25):
• Use of Green Energy & Emissions Reduction: Generated 84.6 MW of wind energy (22%
for captive use), commissioned the Bhagwanpura solar plant at full scale, and expanded
biomass usage including rice husk, mustard husk, and palm fiber as boiler fuel.
Achieved a 23% increase in renewable fuel consumption (Energy basis),
7% reduction in non-renewable fuel use,
7% reduction in Scope 1 & 2 GHG emissions, and
Sequestered 87,336 tCO2 through wind projects.
• Agriculture & Water Management:
Expanded oil palm plantations to 89,546 ha under drip irrigation,
Our Oil Palm Plantation business positively impacted 63,915 farmers—an increase
from over 50,000 in FY24 - through direct procurement of Fresh Fruit Bunches for
Crude Palm Oil processing
Supported oil palm plantation expansion through operation of 49 nurseries, 21 fertilizer
godowns & 173 farmer information cum FFB collection centres across 12 states.
Allocated 202 Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) to empower and support the
agricultural community.
• Community Development: Created 6.77% of jobs in rural India and invested H21.87 crore
in CSR programs focused on health, education, and sports.
• Environmental Stewardship: Increased ZLD plants to 7 sites, with advanced effluent
treatment (MEE/MVR/ATFD) at all other facilities, and promoted organically grown products
in our nutraceutical portfolio.
These accomplishments reflect our enduring commitment to sustainability, operational
excellence, and a positive impact on both environment and society.

I am pleased to present our Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) for the financial year 2024–25, during which
we successfully completed the acquisition of the Home & Personal Care (HPC) Business. This report reflects our commitment to
responsible and sustainable business practices.
We work continuously to embed sustainability into the core of our operations. We set short, medium, and long-term targets and review
progress periodically to ensure steady advancement toward our sustainability goals. In FY 2024–25, we revisited these targets and
achieved several milestones:
Renewable energy: We generated 84.6 MW of wind energy, of which 18.6 MW (22%) was used for our own operations. We also
completed the first year of operations at our Bhagwanpura solar power plant. Renewable energy now contributes 22% of our
operations, supplemented by biomass as renewable fuel across several plants. Collectively, our green energy initiatives led to the
sequestration of 87,336 tonnes of CO2.
Energy efficiency: Targeted measures increased renewable fuel consumption by 23%, reduced non-renewable fuel consumption by
7%, and lowered Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions by 7% compared to the previous year.
Oil palm expansion: Our oil palm plantations now span 89,546 hectares, all equipped with drip irrigation systems. We directly
procured Fresh Fruit Bunches (FFBs) from 63,915 farmers. Supporting infrastructure includes 49 nurseries, 21 fertilizer godowns,
173 collection centres, and partnerships with 202 Farmer Producer Organizations.
Community development: Rural India accounted for 6.77% of total jobs created. We invested H21.87 crore in Corporate Social
Responsibility (CSR) initiatives focused on health, education, and sports.
Environmental stewardship: We expanded Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) facilities to seven sites and ensured all other locations
operate advanced effluent treatment systems. Our nutraceutical business continues to promote organically grown products.
These achievements highlight our commitment to sustainability, operational excellence, and creating long-term value for the
environment and society.
For further details, please refer to the “MD’s Message” section on Page 50 of this Annual Report.

To further strengthen our governance on sustainability and corporate social responsibility,
we have constituted an ESG & CSR Committee (ECC). The ECC is responsible for steering
all sustainability and CSR-related decisions and is chaired by Acharya Balkrishna, Non-
Executive Director, providing strong leadership and dedicated oversight to advance our ESG
commitments.

Yes. All our locations have an occupational health and safety management system in place. Through a comprehensive
Environment, Health & Safety policy, the company lays down its commitment to safe and healthy working conditions.
The policy is available on the company web site www.patanjalifoods.com.
We have implemented a robust Occupational Health and Safety Management System that spans across all our operational
units and offices. Our plants are ISO 45001 certified which covers the Health and Safety hazards with a commitment to
continually improve our health and safety performance and prevention of injuries. We remain dedicated to conducting
operations responsibly to safeguard our employees, the environment, and the communities in which we operate.

We have adopted a comprehensive approach to identify work-related hazards and assess risks in order to maintain
a safe working environment. Our multifaceted process incorporates periodic safety audits, inspections, and Hazard
Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA) techniques.
Our advanced safety management system allows us to identify potential workplace risks and hazards, enabling us to
implement preventive measures and avoid incidents. One of our key tools in maintaining safety is our near-miss reporting.
This encourages employees to report any potential incidents within our premises, allowing us to conduct thorough
investigations, determine causes, and implement corrective actions.
Additionally, we have Environmental, Health, and Safety Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) in place to guide our
operations and ensure the safety of our employees and workers. The primary goal of this control is to create a safe
workplace free from harm, as well as to provide training and an awareness program on important workplace risks by
using the techniques listed below:
1. Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA) is used for routine and non-routine activities, conducted yearly
or as required by any process change.
2. Safety Audits are carried out periodically to identify hazards and the corresponding risks.
3. Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) is used for identifying hazards related to chemical processes.
Furthermore, we have a comprehensive Environment, Health, and Safety (EHS) Policy. The health and safety guidelines
are applicable and followed across all operations. Some of the key processes for identifying work-related hazards and
assessing risks on a routine and non-routine basis are given below:
Pre-start safety review (PSSR) is used and followed for new processes, post any changes/modification activities and
Plant restart up after preventive maintenance.
The online work permit system implemented along with comprehensive JSEA to control non-routine job hazards and
implement control measures against identified risk.
Management of change (MOC) is implemented and followed for all changes including Man, Machine, and Material.
Apart from the above-mentioned measures, to promote effective implementation of control measures, workers are
provided with regular ‘Safety talks & Tool Box Talk’ to communicate available control measures and ensure that all
safety protocols are being followed. Through these consistent efforts, we achieved a significant 70% reduction in
Lost Time Injury (LTI) accidents across group companies, with zero fatal accidents recorded.

We have established robust processes to identify, assess, and manage health and safety hazards across all work sites.
Key practices include:
1. Online platform (EHS Software).
2. Online work permit system, Incident Investigation & CAPA tracker and Safety Observation and Scheduling has been implemented in all units/Plants . This enables us to track and monitor the EHS performance.
3. Monthly safety visits (Safety Walk) by operations team across all the sites and scheduled Audits and Inspections to ensure effective implementation of Safety Management systems.
4. All actionable points are tracked through Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA) /Corrective Action Report for closure and reviewed frequently through online EHS software.
5. Best practices are benchmarked and shared across the business as part of continual improvement.
6. Safety/EHS Alerts are shared with all operational units as part of horizontal deployment and cross-site learning.
7. Fortnightly Safety Musing is shared with all employees.
8. We have integrated a behaviour-based Online Safety Observation Rounds module into our system, enabling online reporting of safe and unsafe practices, conditions and close calls in the workplace. The reported incidents and corrective actions taken are reviewed to determine corresponding remedial measures.
9. We also employ a structured Kaizen scheme that encourages employees at all levels to contribute safety-related suggestions.

The company utilizes a well-defined framework for investigating incidents. When incidents occur, investigations are
triggered, followed by root cause analysis. Response strategies are designed and shared across all facilities to mitigate
the impact. Additionally, the company has a system in place for sharing information regarding significant incidents that
have occurred within group companies to facilitate learning and prevent recurrence. Initial incident investigation reports
are uploaded to the company’s internal EHS software within 24 hours, in line with our safety policy guidelines. In the
case of major incidents, cross-functional teams are deployed to conduct in-depth investigations to find the root cause.
We have a robust audit system that facilitates internal and external audits for Environmental and Occupational Health
Safety parameters throughout the year.
Significant risks/concerns arising from assessment of Health and Safety practices are addressed through elimination
of manual job by use of technology/digitization, safety capability building, monitoring and supervision. Some of the
preventive measures and predictive actions implemented during the reporting period are given below:
Preventive Measures:
1. Departmental ‘On the Job’ training for safety awareness.
2. Daily Toolbox talks in all departments.
3. Awareness through classroom training.
4. Periodic fire drills & mock drills.
5. Effective implementation of safety PPE matrix.
6. Strict safety compliances for critical activities and projects execution work.

We view our stakeholders as integral partners in shaping our strategy and driving sustainable growth. We identify stakeholders based on their relationship and proximity to our operations, their dependence on our business, their ability to influence decisions, and our financial, legal, or ethical responsibilities toward them.

At the core are employees, farmers, suppliers, and customers, who are directly engaged in our value chain. At the next level are investors, governments bodies, and local communities who both influence and are impacted by our activities.
In the wider ecosystem, we engage with industry bodies, NGOs, trade unions, and civil society organizations. We also recognize vulnerable groups, such as women, children, migrant workers, persons with disabilities, and Indigenous communities, who may be disproportionately affected and therefore require focused engagement.
We engage through two-way, inclusive communication channels such as consultations, investor meetings and earnings calls, employee engagement programs, structured feedback processes, and grievance mechanisms.
These efforts help us better understand expectations, address concerns, and proactively resolve issues that may impact long-term value creation.

As part of our commitment to transparency and accountability, we have established strong internal mechanisms to address grievances related to human rights concerns. For employees and workers, we provide multiple accessible channels such as HR Konnect, the Prevention of Sexual Harassment (PoSH) Committee, and dedicated email support, through which concerns can be raised directly or anonymously. In addition, our Code of Conduct and Whistleblower Policy enable individuals across the organization to report issues, including those linked to human rights, without fear of retaliation.
Our grievance redressal framework extends beyond employees to include suppliers, farmers, local communities, and other stakeholders who may be affected by our operations. Dedicated feedback and grievance mechanisms are available at site level and through our community outreach programs, ensuring that concerns from external stakeholders are also heard and addressed.
We place special emphasis on vulnerable groups, such as women, children, migrant workers, and marginalized communities. Mechanisms are designed to remain accessible, confidential, and culturally appropriate, ensuring these groups can raise issues without barriers. For example, grievances can be submitted in local languages, and communitylevel engagement channels provide safe spaces for vulnerable groups to express concerns.
All grievances are formally recorded, reviewed, and investigated by the Human Resources team under the oversight of the Chief Operating Officer (COO). Outcomes and corrective actions are communicated back to the concerned parties, except in cases where anonymity is chosen.
Over time, we plan to implement ISO 26000 on Social Responsibility, establish robust mechanisms to adopt a
comprehensive policy to uphold the rights of all stakeholders.

We made a significant progress in sustainable water management by implementing Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) systems
at seven locations, with plans underway to extend this initiative to additional sites. To minimize environmental impact
and freshwater consumption:
Advanced treatment technologies such as Multiple Effect Evaporators (MEE), Mechanical Vapor Recompression
(MVR), Agitated Thin Film Dryers (ATFD), and Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs) have been installed across all other
manufacturing facilities.
Recycled water is reused for horticulture and industrial purposes, lowering intake from natural freshwater sources.
Solid residues generated through treatment processes are safely managed and disposed of via authorized agencies
in line with environmental standards.
We strictly adhere to all applicable environmental regulations and comply with the standards and directions issued by
the Pollution Control Boards, reaffirming our commitment to sustainable operations and environmental stewardship.

Continuing our commitment to sustainable practices, we strictly adhere to government regulations for the safe and
compliant disposal of hazardous waste. We are focused on minimizing waste generation and disposal by adopting the
“5 R’s” principle: Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repurpose, and Recycle—wherever feasible across our operations.
A variety of initiatives support this waste reduction strategy:In our Palm Plantation business, solid waste such as Empty Fruit Bunches (EFB), fibre, and shells is effectively utilized
as organic fertilizer and fuel, promoting a closed-loop system.
Liquid waste, including Palm Oil Mill Effluent (POME), is sustainably managed to minimize environmental impact.
In the Oleo Chemicals business, byproducts from edible oil processing are used as primary feedstock, reinforcing our
commitment to resource efficiency and circular economy practices.
These integrated waste management practices help us to reduce our environmental footprint and promote long-term
resource sustainability.

As per directives of the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI), we recalled a
specific batch (Batch No. AJD2400012) of our red chilli powder. The recall, was undertaken as
the batch did not meet the prescribed limits for pesticide residues under the Food Safety and Standards (Contaminants, Toxins and Residues) Regulations, 2011.
